title: Chapter Twenty: The Office
content: Seven minutes.

She had been standing outside his office door for seven minutes, and the light underneath it hadn't moved. He was in there. He was waiting. And she was standing in the hallway like a statue, like a woman carved from hesitation, watching the pale rectangle of light on the floorboards as if it might give her permission.
No one was coming to give her permission.

Maeve looked down at herself. Black cardigan, buttoned to the throat. Dark jeans, the ones with the fraying hem she kept meaning to replace. No makeup. Her hair was pulled back in a clip that had lost two of its teeth, strands escaping around her temples. She hadn't dressed up. She wouldn't dress up. If he was going to see her — really see her — it would be this version. The one she'd been hiding behind screen names and typed words for six months. The real one.
The one she could barely stand to look at in mirrors.

She could still turn around. She could walk back down the staircase, past the reference desk, through the oak doors of Harmon Library, and into the October afternoon. She could go home to her apartment with its closed curtains and its silence and its safety, and she could unpick this whole afternoon from her memory like a loose thread.
She could. She could.
Her hand lifted. Knocked. Two knocks, sharp and small, before her courage could retract them.
"Come in."

His voice. Unhurried. Unaware. He didn't know it was her — how could he? She'd sent no message, no warning. Three days of silence since the garden, three days of replaying his voicemail until the words lost meaning from repetition — *You were never invisible to me* — and here she was, turning the handle.

The office smelled of old books and something sharper beneath — sandalwood, she realized. His cologne, faint and lingering, woven into the room like a second occupant. The radiator clanged once, a distant mechanical groan. Late afternoon light fell slantwise through the tall window, catching dust motes suspended in the air, turning them briefly golden.
And there he was.

He was sitting at his desk, surrounded by books — stacked in towers, fanned open, bristling with Post-it flags in a spectrum she recognized as his private indexing system. His face, when he looked up and registered who stood in his doorway, did something complicated. It rearranged itself. Not into the controlled professor mask she'd seen in lecture halls, not into the wounded stranger from the garden. Just — a man. A man who hadn't slept in three days, if the bruises beneath his eyes were any evidence. His collar was wrinkled, half-untucked, the top button undone. His jaw was rough with days of neglected shaving. And there was a stillness to him that looked less like composure and more like something holding together by friction alone.
They stared at each other.

The silence was enormous. It filled the room like water, pressing against the walls, pressing against her ribs. She could hear the radiator tick. She could hear her own pulse, throttling in her ears. She could hear the faint faraway murmur of the library beyond the door — students moving, books being shelved, lives continuing — and the obscene normality of it, the world going on while she stood here drowning in quiet.
Julian spoke first.
"Sit down. Please."

She sat. The chair across from his desk was old, its leather worn smooth by decades of anxious students. It was cold against her back through the cardigan, and she gripped the armrests — the leather cracking softly under her fingers — because her hands needed something to hold or they would shake.

He didn't sit. He stood and moved to the window, turning his back to her, looking out at the quad where the last of the ivy was going red. The glass threw a pale version of his reflection toward her, ghostly and incomplete.
"I owe you an apology," he said.
She blinked. "What?"
He still didn't turn. "I spent six months falling in love with someone's mind." The word *love* landed in the room like a stone in still water. "And when I found whose mind it was, I said—"
*"It was you."*
She heard it again, the way he'd said it in the garden. The way it had sounded like a gavel.

"—'It was you.' As if it was an accusation." He paused. The window was cold; she could see the faint fog of his breath against the glass. "It wasn't. It was recognition. And I'm sorry I didn't make that clear in the garden."
Maeve's response came out barely louder than breathing. "You said it like a verdict."

That turned him. He moved from the window, and the cold came with him — she felt it trace across her skin as he passed, the draft from the old glass following in his wake. He stood between the desk and the chair where she sat, and his eyes found her face.
"No," he said quietly. "I said it like a discovery. Like finding a room in a house you thought you knew."

His eyes were on her face. On *her* face. Not on the version of her that existed in typed paragraphs and midnight messages, not on the mind she'd offered him from behind a curtain of anonymity. On her actual face, with its uneven skin and its propensity for flushing and its complete inability to revise itself before being seen.
And for the first time, she didn't look away.
She let him look.

It was the hardest thing she'd ever done. Harder than sending that first message six months ago. Harder than every reply that followed, each one a strip of herself laid bare on the page. Those had cost something, but they'd cost it at a distance, in private, in the safe dark of her own room where no eyes were on her body, where no one could measure the words against the vessel that held them and find the vessel wanting.
This — his eyes on her face, her not hiding — this was something else. This was standing still while the blade fell.

"I lied to you," she said. The words came out stronger than she expected, as if the admission itself had weight, as if it could anchor her. "For six months. Every word I wrote was true and every name I signed was false."
He nodded. "I know."
"Aren't you angry?"

He considered this. Not performatively — not the way people pause before a rehearsed answer. He actually turned the question over, examining it the way he examined texts in his seminars, looking for the thing beneath the surface. She watched him think. She had always loved watching him think. Even now. Especially now.

"I should be," he said finally. "Part of me is. The deception — the mechanics of it — that's not nothing. You let me believe I was corresponding with someone else. With— " He stopped himself. Whatever name she'd used, whatever constructed identity, it hung unspoken between them.

"But," he continued, and the word was quiet, almost gentle, "the words were true. You just said so. And I fell in love with the words. With the mind behind them." His voice caught, barely, on *fell* — the past tense refusing to stay past. "That mind is real. That mind is yours. The name..."

He trailed off. His hand found the edge of the desk, fingers pressing into the wood as if he needed the contact, the grounding. She could smell the books around them — that particular dusty, sweet decay of old paper and binding glue — mingled with his cologne, and the combination was so specific, so *him*, that it made her chest ache.
"The name was wrong," he said. "But the love wasn't."
The word — *love* — in the same room as her body.
Maeve flinched.

It was involuntary, a small recoil in the chair, her fingers tightening on the worn leather armrests. She saw him see it. His expression shifted — not hurt, not frustration. Something more like grief. Something that said: *of course. Of course you flinched. Of course that's where you are.*
His voice softened. "You still don't believe me."
It wasn't a question, but she answered anyway. "I don't know what to believe."

"Yes, you do. You believe I'm wrong. You think this is conditional." He straightened, moved closer — not crowding, but near enough that she could see the tiny muscles move in his jaw, the way his throat worked. "You think that if you stand up and I see all of you—" He gestured, a sweep of his hand that meant her whole body, her whole self, everything she kept hidden behind desk edges and cardigan buttons and careful camera angles— "I'll change my mind."
She nodded.

Because she did believe that. She believed it in her marrow, in the calcium of her bones, in the place deeper than thought where the body keeps its certainties. She believed that what she was on the page and what she was in the flesh were two irreconcilable things, and that no one — not anyone, and certainly not him — could hold both at once without dropping one.

It was the most fundamental fact she possessed. More fundamental than her name, which she'd proven willing to abandon. More fundamental than her scholarship, which she could have abandoned too. She could not believe — *would not* let herself believe — that he could see all of her and stay.
"So you came here," Julian said, "to stand in my office and prove yourself right."

"I came here because I—" She stopped. The sentence fractured. What came after? Because she what? Because she wanted him to prove her wrong? Because she was tired of hiding? Because his voice on that voicemail — *You were never invisible to me* — had cracked something open inside her that she couldn't seal shut again, and the draft from it was cold, and she was so goddamn tired of being cold?
"I don't know why I came," she said.
"I think you do."

She shook her head. Her hair had escaped the clip entirely now; she could feel it against her neck, damp at the roots. The room was too warm from the radiator, yet the cold from the window still crept across the floor. She existed in two temperatures at once, and wasn't that just perfect.
Julian did something she didn't expect.

He turned to his desk and picked up a sheaf of papers — printed, dog-eared, marked with his familiar blue pen annotations in the margins. Her paper. The one on epistolary intimacy, the one she'd submitted for his seminar last semester, the one where she'd argued — with the ferocity and precision of someone who believed every word — that the letter form created a kind of knowing that the body could not achieve.
He read aloud.

"'The epistolary form demands a vulnerability that the body resists. To write is to undress the mind. To receive is to witness the undressing. The deepest intimacy may not be physical at all — it may be the act of being read.'"

His voice was steady. Not performative. Not the resonant lecture-hall projection he used on crowds. Just his voice, in his office, reading her words back to her, and each syllable landed on her skin like something lit.
He set the paper down. The soft slap of it against the desk was the loudest sound in the room.
"You wrote that." His eyes found hers again, held them. "You *wrote* that. And you don't believe your own thesis?"
She opened her mouth. No sound came out.
Because he was right.

She had written those words — *to write is to undress the mind* — and she had meant them, entirely and completely, at the time of writing. She had believed, in the act of composition, that the exposure of the self through language was a form of intimacy as profound as any physical act. She had argued it with evidence and conviction and the particular passion of someone who knows, instinctively, that she is describing something true.
She had written the proof of her own possibility.
And she had never — not once, not for a single moment — believed it applied to her.
The silence stretched. The radiator ticked. Somewhere in the library, a door closed.

Maeve sat in the worn leather chair, and the cold from the window pressed against her back, and the smell of old books and his cologne filled her lungs, and she had no answer. Not for him. Not for herself. Not for the woman who had written those words with such certainty and then spent every day since proving, with the architecture of her hiding, that she didn't believe a single one of them.
Julian wasn't looking away from her. He was waiting. Patiently, impossibly, as if he had nothing but time, as if the world outside this office had kindly consented to stop.

She could still stand up. She could still walk out. She could retreat to the safety of what she knew — which was that she was too much, too visible, too bound to a body that didn't match the voice she'd found in words — and the door would close behind her, and they would both be safe in their separate silences.
Or she could stay.
She could stay and find out what happened when the woman who wrote about undressing the mind finally let herself be seen.
Her hands were still gripping the armrests. Her knuckles were white. She didn't let go.
But she didn't stand up either.
